title: From "LLMs Can't Price" to 1,686 Lines of Mechanical Execution
summary: OPC v0.10b's core insight: LLMs can't do cardinal pricing, but they excel at ordinal pattern recognition. One session turned spec into code—10 red flags, gate logic, delta comparison, criteria lint, all mechanized.
body:
I first realized the WTP gate was a dead end when I brought in 4 AI reviewers for cross-evaluation.

An agent posing as a senior with 12 years of experience said: **MAJOR_REVISION_NEEDED, cut 70% of the mechanism**. An agent playing indie founder said: **can only work as regression detector, not WTP gate**. The devil's advocate was more direct: **HIGHLY_EXPLOITABLE, 12 specific attack vectors**.

What got rejected wasn't the details—it was the entire premise.

## The problem: cardinal vs ordinal

OPC's previous UX quality judgment followed the WTP (Willingness-to-Pay) approach—having LLMs simulate users answering "How much would you pay for this product?" Sounds reasonable, but it's asking a probability model to do something it fundamentally can't: generate cardinal economic signals.

The gap between $5 and $50 isn't "a difference in probability distributions"—it's different positions on an entire demand elasticity curve. LLMs don't have ground truth for that curve. Making them output dollar amounts is like asking someone who's never eaten to rate restaurants.

But LLMs **can** do ordinal judgments. They can say "this is better than that," spot "broken link," identify "missing loading feedback." Pattern recognition, relative comparison, pattern matching—that's transformer sweet spot territory.

So v0.10b's core pivot: **Don't ask "what's it worth"—ask "does it have these 10 red flags."**

## 10 red flags as closed enum

This isn't an open list. It's a closed enum, 10 keys, each key with different severity across three tiers (functional / polished / delightful):

```javascript
export const RED_FLAGS = [
  { key: "default-favicon",      severity: { functional: null,         polished: "warning",  delightful: "critical" } },
  { key: "stack-trace-visible",  severity: { functional: "warning",    polished: "critical", delightful: "critical" } },
  { key: "broken-link",          severity: { functional: "critical",   polished: "critical", delightful: "critical" } },
  { key: "data-loss-on-error",   severity: { functional: "critical",   polished: "critical", delightful: "critical" } },
  // ... 6 more
];
```

`default-favicon` is null at functional tier (don't care), becomes warning at polished, becomes critical at delightful. Same pattern, different standards. This is 100x more useful than "rate it out of 10"—because you know **exactly what to fix**.

Interestingly, this enum was calibrated using my own dream-works portfolio as ground truth. Took 15 Vercel apps, screenshot by screenshot, labeled red flags, compared trust signals. Turned out 4 apps hadn't even changed their favicon—stuff like that doesn't need LLM pricing, mechanical detection is enough.

## From spec to code: three bugs and one architectural decision

The protocol docs (`ux-simulation-protocol.md` etc.) were written last session. This session was about turning spec into code that `opc-harness` can actually run.

Sounds like grunt work, but hit three gotchas.

### Bug 1: `!sections["Out of Scope"]` false positive on empty strings

JavaScript's falsy trap. When `## Out of Scope` is at EOF with no content following, `extractSections` captures an empty string `""`. But `!""` is `true`. So a section that **actually exists** gets flagged as "missing."

```javascript
// Before — would false-flag empty sections as missing
if (!sections["Out of Scope"]) { fail(...) }

// After — only fail if truly absent
if (sections["Out of Scope"] === undefined) { fail(...) }
```

All four section checks had this issue. One implicit falsy conversion affecting 4 structural checks.

### Bug 2: `OUT-1:` prefix numbers trigger HAS_MEASUREMENT

`criteria-lint`'s vague outcome detection logic: if contains vague word (fast, clean, intuitive...) but no measurement, fail. `HAS_MEASUREMENT` uses `\d` to match numbers.

Problem: the `1` in `OUT-1: API is fast` gets matched by `\d`. So lint thinks "has number = has measurement" and lets an obviously vague outcome slide.

Fix was brutal: strip `OUT-N:` prefixes before analysis.

```javascript
const contentText = out.text.replace(/^OUT-\d+:\s*/, "");
```

### Bug 3: `parseRedFlagOverrides` used `await import` in non-async function

Originally wanted to use dynamic import to read override files in `tier-baselines.mjs`. But this module is pure data + pure functions, no async context.

Final architectural decision: **modules don't do I/O**. `parseRedFlagOverrides(content)` only accepts text content, I/O is caller's job (`ux-verdict.mjs`). Preserved tier-baselines' pure function nature.

This wasn't "fixing a bug"—it was making an architectural choice: data modules never touch the filesystem.

## Gate logic: two paths

`ux-verdict`'s core is gate logic. Not complex, but two scenarios:

**First run** (no baseline):
- critical ≥ 1 → FAIL
- warning > threshold → ITERATE
- tier_fit = free-only or below-tier → ITERATE
- otherwise → PASS

**Subsequent run** (has baseline delta):
- critical ≥ 1 → FAIL
- has regression → FAIL
- has improvement + warning ≤ threshold → PASS
- has improvement + warning > threshold → ITERATE
- no change + warning > threshold → ITERATE
- no change + warning ≤ threshold → PASS

Warning threshold is tier-parameterized: functional=3, polished=2, delightful=1. Higher standards are stricter.

This logic is about 20 lines of code. But tests have 44 assertions—including edge cases, delta regression, override activation, tier switching affecting same flag's severity.

## Observer schema validation: 7 mechanical rejections

UX observer output is JSON. But LLM-generated JSON isn't always reliable. So `ux-verdict` does 7 schema validations before aggregation:

1. Missing top-level field → BLOCKED
2. red_flag key not in enum (and not `other`) → BLOCKED
3. `other` without description → BLOCKED
4. tier_fit not in valid set → BLOCKED
5. friction_point without reference → BLOCKED
6. reasoning < 40 chars → BLOCKED
7. reasoning uses third person ("users would", "people find") → BLOCKED

#7 is interesting. UX observer is required to speak **first person**—"I think this loads too slowly" not "users might think it loads slowly." Third person means the observer is **fabricating** rather than **experiencing**. Classic LLM laziness signal.

Any single failure means entire verdict is BLOCKED. Not warning—direct rejection.

## Criteria lint: 14 DoD mechanical checks

Another new command. Had a key discussion earlier—"DoD is the control plane for the entire pipeline." What are evaluators scoring? What standards are implementers building to? If acceptance criteria themselves are vague, every downstream step is spinning wheels.

So `criteria-lint` doesn't allow vague criteria. 14 checks:

**7 structural**: outcomes exist, count 3-7, verification exists, each outcome has corresponding verification, quality constraints exist, scope exists, tier section exists (if tier specified)

**4 content**: vague word detection (fast/clean/intuitive... without measurement = fail), impossible-to-fail detection ("should work as expected" = fail), manual-only verification detection, outcome deduplication (Jaccard > 0.8 = fail)

**3 warnings**: empty scope, no failure mode outcomes, outcomes > 5

Jaccard similarity deduplication is an interesting choice. Not exact matching—word set intersection/union. Two outcomes with 80% identical words are basically duplicates—merge or differentiate.

## Final numbers

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| CLI commands | 20 | 23 |
| Lib modules | 14 | 17 |
| Test suites | 16 | 18 |
| Total assertions | 340 | 408 |
| New code | — | 1,686 lines |

18 suites / 408 assertions / 0 failures.

---

## Honest assessment

What this session did was essentially **move rules from docs into code**. Spec files were written last session, this was about making them machine-executable.

The good parts: red flag enum is closed, gate logic is deterministic, schema validation is mechanical. None of this depends on LLM subjective judgment. OPC's UX quality checking went from "human glances and thinks it's fine" to "18 test suites run and tell you if it's fine."

The not-so-good parts: the entire system hasn't been battle-tested by real OPC loops yet. Red flag detection should theoretically catch default favicons and broken links, but will LLM observers faithfully execute the protocol in actual sessions? Third person detection and reasoning length checks are safeguards, not proof.

There's a more fundamental question: are these 10 red flags enough? They're extracted from manual annotation of 15 dream-works apps—not a huge sample. The `other` key is an escape hatch, but if `other` gets heavy usage, it means the enum is insufficient.

Next step is running a real project through the complete UX simulation loop—from acceptance criteria to observer reports to verdict. See if the mechanical layer converges or oscillates in the wild.
